chunks.content:
This ‘explosion’ of generative AI applications has caused
many world leaders to call for a proper debate on the
opportunities and challenges related to generative
AI and the regulatory needs and guidelines required
for its safe and responsible use. The UK government
therefore hosted the world’s first AI Safety Summit in
November 2023.
chunks.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><RIGHT>This ‘explosion’ of generative AI applications has caused</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>many world leaders to call for a proper debate on the</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>opportunities and challenges related to generative</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>AI and the regulatory needs and guidelines required</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>for its safe and responsible use. The UK government</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>therefore hosted the world’s first AI Safety Summit in</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><CENTER>November 2023.</CENTER></LINE></TEXT>

children.content:
Many countries are currently leveraging their existing
legal and policy frameworks to manage the risks of
AI, whilst in Europe the EU is advancing a continent
wide ‘AI Act’ to responsibly seize the opportunities
presented by AI. Many other multinational groups
including several UN bodies, the G7 and private
sector companies are also developing responsible AI
guidelines. These are discussed further in the ‘AI Risks
& Governance’ report from WTTC.
children.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><RIGHT>Many countries are currently leveraging their existing</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>legal and policy frameworks to manage the risks of</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>AI, whilst in Europe the EU is advancing a continent</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>wide ‘AI Act’ to responsibly seize the opportunities</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>presented by AI. Many other multinational groups</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>including several UN bodies, the G7 and private</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>sector companies are also developing responsible AI</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><RIGHT>guidelines. These are discussed further in the ‘AI Risks</RIGHT></LINE><LINE><CENTER>& Governance’ report from WTTC.</CENTER></LINE></TEXT>

children.content:
In September 2023, an OECD report prepared for the 2023 Japanese G7 Presidency identified seven common
challenges posed by generative AI that require international alignment and collaboration 23. The report noted
that international policy frameworks for generative AI should be inclusive and feature input from a diverse
range of stakeholders, not just ‘AI experts’ and include the perspectives of governments from both developed
and developing countries, private companies, civil society and interested sectors of the economy. This includes
international Travel & Tourism.
children.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><CENTER>In September 2023, an OECD report prepared for the 2023 Japanese G7 Presidency identified seven common</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>challenges posed by generative AI that require international alignment and collaboration 23. The report noted</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>that international policy frameworks for generative AI should be inclusive and feature input from a diverse</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>range of stakeholders, not just ‘AI experts’ and include the perspectives of governments from both developed</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>and developing countries, private companies, civil society and interested sectors of the economy. This includes</CENTER></LINE><LINE><LEFT>international Travel & Tourism.</LEFT></LINE></TEXT>

children.content: GLOBAL DIGITAL DIVIDE & AI SKILLS GAP

As noted earlier in this chapter, as AI systems access more high quality data and use greater computer power,
they become more accurate, creative and insightful. However it is also important to note that handling massive
amounts of data can present privacy, security and data governance challenges for AI developers and users and
that there is a significant data and ‘digital divide’ in the world.
children.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><CENTER>As noted earlier in this chapter, as AI systems access more high quality data and use greater computer power,</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>they become more accurate, creative and insightful. However it is also important to note that handling massive</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>amounts of data can present privacy, security and data governance challenges for AI developers and users and</CENTER></LINE><LINE><LEFT>that there is a significant data and ‘digital divide’ in the world.</LEFT></LINE></TEXT>

children.content:
The International Telecommunications Union (ITU) – the UN agency responsible for advancing digital technology
– estimates that 34% of the world has never been connected to the internet (in 2022), representing 2.6
billion people 25. Therefore a third of the world’s population does not have access to AI.
children.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><CENTER>The International Telecommunications Union (ITU) – the UN agency responsible for advancing digital technology</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>– estimates that 34% of the world has never been connected to the internet (in 2022), representing 2.6</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>billion people 25. Therefore a third of the world’s population does not have access to AI.</CENTER></LINE></TEXT>

children.content:
The ITU considers that ‘universal and meaningful digital connectivity’ 26 (defined as a ‘the possibility for
everyone to enjoy a safe, productive and affordable online experience’) should be a global priority. The ITU
states that the AI revolution should benefit everyone in the world and should not worsen the international
digital divide.
children.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><CENTER>The ITU considers that ‘universal and meaningful digital connectivity’ 26 (defined as a ‘the possibility for</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>everyone to enjoy a safe, productive and affordable online experience’) should be a global priority. The ITU</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>states that the AI revolution should benefit everyone in the world and should not worsen the international</CENTER></LINE><LINE><LEFT>digital divide.</LEFT></LINE></TEXT>

children.content:
ITU data shows that the global youth are the driving force behind connectivity, with 75% of all 15-24 year olds
in the world now online, compared to 65% amongst the rest of the population. Sadly the affordability of entry
level fixed and mobile internet services is leaving populations in the least developed countries (LDCs) behind.
children.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><CENTER>ITU data shows that the global youth are the driving force behind connectivity, with 75% of all 15-24 year olds</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>in the world now online, compared to 65% amongst the rest of the population. Sadly the affordability of entry</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>level fixed and mobile internet services is leaving populations in the least developed countries (LDCs) behind.</CENTER></LINE></TEXT>

children.content:
ITU data from 2022 finds that in a typical low income economy, the cheapest mobile broadband costs 9% of a
person’s income (over 6x the global average) and in those least developed countries 64% of the population is
not connected to the internet (almost double the global average of 34% who are not online).
children.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><CENTER>ITU data from 2022 finds that in a typical low income economy, the cheapest mobile broadband costs 9% of a</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>person’s income (over 6x the global average) and in those least developed countries 64% of the population is</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>not connected to the internet (almost double the global average of 34% who are not online).</CENTER></LINE></TEXT>

children.content:
To begin to address this issue, the UK and Canada announced an ‘AI for Development’ vision at the 2023 UN
General Assembly (UNGA) 27, which aims to boost local AI skills, innovation and computing power in the
world’s poorest nations, with an initial focus on Africa. Then UK Foreign Secretary, James Cleverly stated at the
UN launch that “the opportunity of AI is immense…and the UK alongside our allies and partners is making sure
that the fulfilment of this enormous potential is shared globally”
